text: This video demonstrates single sitting robotic retroperitoneal pelvic and inguinal lymphadenectomy. A fifty seven year old gentleman presented with enlarged hard and fixed right inguinal lymph nodes. The true cut biopsy revealed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. He had a prior history of excision of eccrine carcinoma of the right flank in two thousand nine. He was disease free on yearly follow ups.

A PET-CT scan was done, which revealed metastasis in parietic atriocaval, retrocaval, paracavel, right common iliac, right inguinal, and left cervical lymph nodes. The cytology from left cervical node was suggestive of squamous carcinomatous deposits.

In view of the good performance status of the patient and positive treatment guidelines for this extremely rare neoplasm, it was decided to proceed with simultaneous robotic retroperitoneal right pelvic and right inguinal lymphadenectomy. An informed consent was obtained from the patient regarding the non-curative nature of the surgery.

The da Vinci X-ray system was used for the procedure. The patient was placed in the Tenderlenburg position for the retropaternal and the pelvic part of the surgery. A split leg position was employed for the inguinal lymphadenectomy.

The surgical team places ports in a horizontal straight line for the bilateral retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND), including a 12mm assistant port on the right side and another 12mm port in the suprapubic area over the midline to assist with the left RPLND and manage potential vascular injuries.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum medial to the cecum and lifts the peritoneal folds using external nylon sutures on straight needles. The right common iliac lymph nodes are dissected up to the vena cava. A rent on the anterior aspect of the vena cava is visible and repaired using 3-0 proline sutures. The dissection then continues cranially.

The surgeon uses the split and roll method to separate lymph nodes from major vessels. Lumbar vessels are divided between hemlock clips. Paracaval lymph nodes are dissected and removed en bloc. Dissection of intra-aortocaval and pre-aortic lymph nodes is performed, during which the left renal artery is injured at the ostium.

The surgeon uses the left robotic instrument to apply compression on the site of the injury while repairing the rent with a rescue stitch made from 3-0 Prolene suture. An intraoperative Doppler ultrasound is performed, confirming the restoration of pulsatile flow in the left renal artery following the repair.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to divide small vessels and lymphatic channels between clips. The para-aortic lymph nodes are further dissected. During the procedure, the inferior mesenteric artery is inadvertently injured and subsequently clipped. The ostium of the artery is repaired with a three-oh proline rescue stitch.

The surgeon loops the aorta and dissects the inter-aortocaval lymph nodes, proceeding cranially to identify the right renal artery. The inter-aortocaval lymph nodes are removed, followed by deeper dissection to remove the retrocaval and paracaval lymph nodes. The final view shows the great vessels after the completed retroperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon rotates the boom towards the right pelvis using a 30-degree up camera angle to perform an extended dissection of the right pelvic lymph nodes. Grossly enlarged lymph nodes surrounding the iliac artery and vein are dissected and removed. The dissection continues distally over the iliac vessels, culminating in a final view of the right pelvis post-lymphadenectomy.

The robotic system is repositioned to subcutaneous ports for the right inguinal lymphadenectomy. Dissection begins superficially to the fascia lata, extending to the inguinal ligament. The fascia lata is opened to expose the femoral vessels. The tributaries and main trunk of the great saphenous vein are divided. An accidental tear in the right femoral artery is repaired using a three-oh proline rescue stitch.

The surgeon continues cranial dissection while carefully avoiding thinning and button-holing of the skin. The lymph node at the clock weight is removed and placed into a specimen bag. The femoral ring is closed to complete the procedure.

The total consult time was four sixty-five minutes and the estimated blood loss was three hundred milliliters. The patient did not require any intraoperative or postoperative blood transfusion. He was discharged on the third postoperative day with a groin drain which was removed after twelve days. Seventy-eight out of the seventy-nine lymph nodes removed were positive for poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The patient was started on cycloposphamide, adriamycin, cisplatin chemotherapy and is doing well at two months of follow-up.

This video is presented to emphasize the minimally invasive management of vascular complications of retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy and inguinal lymphadenectomy. These complications can be very well managed using the robot without compromising the oncological outcomes. The importance of a solid and skilled robotic team cannot be underestimated.
